content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
November 10, 1976


Hannah-Beth Jackson
Law Clerk, Elections Law
Office of the District Attorney
County of Santa Barbara
118 E. Figueroa St.
Santa Barbara, CA 95101

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 76-055

Dear Ms. Jackson:

     In its November meeting, the Fair Political Practices
Commission instructed the staff to send you this letter in
lieu of the formal opinion you requested (No. 76-055).  This
action was based on the fact that we have been advised that
your office no longer is contemplating filing an action
against the University of California based on activities in
connection with a county election held in March, 1975, and
that the question you posed concerning who would be the
civil prosecutor for such an action, therefore, is moot.

     In general, we think that under Government Code
Section 91001(b) the district attorney is the civil prose-
cutor for violations of the Political Reform Act which occur
in connection with county elections.  However, whether this
will be the case when a state agency is the alleged violator
presents an interpretive question which the Commission does
not want to address in a hypothetical context.  See 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18320(g)(4).

     Thank you for your interest in the Political Reform
Act.  If you have any further questions, please contact Ted
Prim, an attorney with the Commission's Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director
